pages.raw_text:
United Mine Workers headquar.
tiers, Greensburg, Pa.


Perhaps you read that story, and
if you did you undoubtedly felt sor
ry for the strikers and their FAINT
lies. You may have wished that
you could help them, but did you
after having partaken OF your
Thanksgiving dinner, give those
unfortunates a second thought, and
try to devise some way in which
you could be of assistance to them :


| didn't.


But A. Excl did.


You probably don't know A. Excl
| never saw him until last even
ing, when he came to my home


II want to see you," he said as
opened the door in answer to his
knock.


Il thought maybe you could send
some money to these folks" he
said, in reply to my question as to
what he wanted.


Then l asked him to come in and
explain, for A. Excl talks rather
broken English, and l did not un
derstand what he wanted.


He came in, and took from his
pocket a clipping OF the Greens
burg story published in The Press


Il read this" he said. II read
how boy, eight years. have ut
shoes; how girl have no shoes. and
think maybe you would send EN-
five dollars to get some. I been
there. l know what it is like and
want to help some. l think you get
money there quicker than me-"


He handed me the clipping and
a again read how at Jamison camp
No. 1, an eight-year-old boy limpeo
around in the half-frozen slush
bare-footed, his feet in terrible con
